headlines.title: San Jose community mobilizes to support victims of Venezuela earthquakes
headlines.description: Watching the devastation in Venezuela has prompted dozens of people throughout the Bay Area to spring into action to donate relief supplies. On Saturday, there was a donation drive at Arepas Latin Cuisine, a Venezuelan restaurant with locations in San Francisco and San Jose; many who went to the San Jose location to donate said they have relatives in Venezuela, and they are heartbroken the death toll is rising. Dorly Lopez’s cousins live in Venezuela, so for her, the mission is personal. “They have to move. And some fear there is going to be a tsunami,” she said. Lopez stopped at the restaurant with full arms and a heavy heart. Venezuela Jun 25 Venezuela Jun 27 Venezuela Jun 25 “We want to help. we see the disaster in Venezuela we are heartbroken,” she said. Lopez was among many others who were dropping off sleeping pads, clothing, water, first aid kits and diapers at the restaurant. There were so many donations that employees had to stuff the items into a closet and a van. Not only is Arepas collecting supplies, but the restaurant is also donating a portion of the profits from meals served here to relief efforts in Venezuela. For Marianny Mata, watching the devastation from the quake has been especially painful. “I was born and raised in Venezuela and my family lives there,” she said. Mata worries about those close to her. “My godmother… her uncle is missing. He’s been missing and they still haven’t found him,” she said. A video from Caracas captured the moment buildings collapsed following two back-to-back magnitude seven plus quakes, prompting the Arepas restaurant in San Francisco to also collect supplies for families in need. “It made me feel horrible made me feel responsible. I just really need to do something,” restaurant owner Joanna Torres said. As the frantic search for survivors continues in Venezuela, more than four thousand miles away people in the Bay Area are answering the call to help. Medical, baby supplies and nonperishable food were top priorities. The first shipment of donations was expected to head to the Red Cross in Caracas on Tuesday.

headlines.title: Violent incidents during Pride celebrations in San Francisco bring concerns
headlines.description: The San Francisco Pride weekend is in full swing, but a few violent incidents, including a shooting near an entrance in the Castro District, vandalism, and two cases where State Senator Scott Wiener said he was “harassed, threated and intimidated” are bringing concerns. That violence is catching the attention of millions of people on social media, on a weekend when the world’s eyes are on the Bay Area for Pride and World Cup. One of those incidents was posted on social media. The viral video shows a crowd during Friday’s Trans March shouting at State Senator and Congressional Candidate Scott Wiener, eventually chasing him away. After having a day to process what happened, Wiener said he felt “harassed, threatened and intimidated.” “But when folks are basically swarming around you, getting really close, and then touching you, and just trying to drive you out of the park physically, that’s not okay. That crosses a line,” he said. San Francisco 21 hours ago San Francisco 18 hours ago Wiener says some people were actually getting physical with him, and for the first time in 22 years, he left the march because he didn’t feel safe. “Which makes me really sad, because it’s the first Trans March I’ve ever missed since it started in 2004, but it would not have been safe for me or for my team,” he said. Wiener says he and his staff were also accosted in a separate incident while attending a World Cup watch party Wednesday night in the Mission District. “A guy who had previously harassed me, in a very antisemitic way, sort of cornered me, and blocked me and was getting very close to me and being very aggressive,” he said. On Friday’s, the incident that occurred during the Trans March left to San Francisco police officers injured and five people under arrest. SFPD released a statement, which in part read: “The SFPD always respects individuals First Amendment rights to protest; however, criminal activity will not be tolerated in San Francisco.” Pride participants told NBC Bay Area they’re aware of what happened, but not too worried. “You can’t, and that kind of violence and threat of intimidation because if you stay away because you’re afraid something might happen they’ve already won,” San Francisco resident Sister Roma said. “I love being here. I love seeing everybody, seeing everybody happy. This is what pride is about. It’s just about the beauty of being in community and just being together with people and feeling that love,” ally Jason said. Wiener agrees with them and SFPD. He says he respects everyone’s rights to disagree and voice their own political views, but he says too often, people are crossing the line into harassment, intimidation and bullying. “We need to fight them, and stick up for each other, and that’s what Pride is about. It’s about celebrating the community and recommitting to the fight ahead,” he said. The main Pride weekend event, the San Francisco Pride Parade and Celebration, will be held Sunday morning. Wiener said he will be there.

headlines.title: Person arrested after shooting near Pride festivities in San Francisco
headlines.description: A person was arrested and accused of shooting another near the Pride festivities in San Francisco Saturday, according to police. Police said at around 3:30 p.m., officers were at the United Nations Plaza are monitoring Pride events when a shooting occurred. Officers found a victim suffering from gunshot wounds and rendered aid, according to the department. Authorities said the victim was taken to a local hospital for non-life-threatening injuries. Officers in the area found a possible suspect and foot pursuit ensued, police said. Authorities said they were able to apprehend him for the investigation, and he was placed under arrest. A police officer resulted injured from the pursuit, and was taken to the hospital, according to authorities. The police department said this remains an active investigation and encourages anyone with information to contact them at 415-575-4444 or text a tip to TIP411 and begin the message with SFPD.

headlines.title: Authorities investigate fire at San Jose school
headlines.description: An investigation is underway following a fire at Blackford Elementary School in San Jose Saturday, authorities said. According to San Jose Fire, the incident was reported at around 2:25 p.m. The flames were contained to one room and knocked down at 2:40 p.m., according to authorities. No injuries were reported, and the fire department said their Arson Unit is investigating origin and cause.

headlines.title: Investigation into possible shooting leads to standoff in San Francisco
headlines.description: An investigation into a possible shooting led to a police standoff Saturday on Eddy Street in San Francisco. Police said they responded to the 100 block of Eddy Street at around 1:45 p.m. for a possible shooting. At the scene, officers said they were trying to meet with the occupant of a room, but they refused to exit and comply with their demands. The police department said additional resources responded to the scene to obtain a peaceful resolution.

headlines.title: Blind teen from Berkeley competes in national Braille Challenge Finals
headlines.description: A blind seventh grader from Berkeley competed in the national Braille Challenge Finals in Los Angeles this weekend, winning first place in the “Charts and Graphs” category. Kai Wang is one of only 50 competitors and the only one from California in the challenge. The competition tests the ability to read, comprehend and write braille. Technology has led to a decrease in the use of braille over the years, but Wang believes it still serves an important role in the visually impaired community. “I think braille is important because its literacy. It helps people learn not only reading and writing. But all other subjects, math, science, even music. And I think it’s also, very important to meet other people who value braille literacy as much as I do in this event,” he said. Kai, who is 13 years old, goes to Longview Middle School. According to his parents, he is the first braille-only student to enroll in Berkeley public schools in decades. Wang spoke with NBC Bay Area’s Garvin Thomas about his participation. Watch the full interview in the video above.

headlines.title: 5 arrested after vandalism during Trans March in San Francisco
headlines.description: Vandalism along the route of the San Francisco Trans March on Friday led to five arrests and a scuffle that injured two officers, police said. Officers monitoring the march about 7 p.m. saw people vandalize property with paint at Market and 8th streets, said Officer Robert Rueca, a department spokesman. One of the people also assaulted and sprayed paint on a person, Rueca said on social media. Officers attempted to detain suspects at Turk and Taylor streets, but other people surrounded and obstructed them in an attempt to free the people, Rueca said. Police arrested three people for assault and vandalism and two for obstructing the officers’ Two officers were assaulted and received medical treatment for non-life-threatening injuries, Rueca said. “The SFPD always respects individuals First Amendment rights to protest,” Rueca said. “However, criminal activity will not be tolerated in San Francisco.” He also said the SFPD “is unwavering in its support for the LGBTQ+ community and will continue to protect and serve every community in San Francisco.” Anyone with information on the incident is asked to contact San Francisco police at 415-575-4444 or send a text to TIP411 and begin the message with SFPD.

headlines.title: Firefighters battle brush fire in Antioch
headlines.description: Firefighters are battling a wildfire in Antioch Friday that has burned over 10 acres, according to authorities. The incident, dubbed the Deer Fire, started just after 2:30 pm. in the area of Deer Valley Road and Deer Hill Lane, according to the Cal Fire website. The fire was burning 30 acres, but it later shrunk to 15 acres, according to authorities. Fire officials said some strcutures were threatened at one point, but they are no longer in danger. At around 10:30 p.m, officials said the forward progress stopped. The cause of the fire is unknown.

headlines.title: Fans excited ahead of U.S.-Bosnia and Herzegovina match in Santa Clara
headlines.description: U.S. soccer fans are expected to bring a lot of business and enthusiasm to the Bay Area next week, as the USMNT takes on Bosnia and Herzegovina in the Round of 32 of the World Cup. Watch parties for the July 1 game are already planned for fans on both sides of the match; one of those will be hosted by the American Outlaws soccer group at Jack’s Bar and Lounge in San Jose. The American Outlaws have also been helping get shuttles for their members to the games and with booking hotels. They say hotels and shuttle reservations are filling up fast, and they will march to the stadium the day of the game. This is just a small sample of what the American Outlaws expect for the July 1 watch party; a surreal experience for one of the organizers, including Jordan Trigg, a long-time U.S. soccer fan who helped form a local “American Outlaws” chapter in San Jose a decade ago. “To see it all come to fruition and actually and have the US team playing here next week – it’s once in a lifetime,” Trigg said. Trigg says he’ll be at the game with his family, and will be with the American Outlaw team USA fans when they perform their traditional march into the stadium. “The march is going to be chanting the whole time, scarfs waving, collecting people along the way – and it’s just going to get that enthusiasm going,” he said. Bosnia-Herzegovina fans say just being here is a win. “I love it. We already win. We have nothing to lose,” Bosnia fan Ferid Tabakovic said. “I go to every game. I already bought tickets for Santa Clara,” Bosnia fan Sead Tucakovic said. They’re not expected to attract nearly as many fans to the bay area for the game, but they will bring plenty of enthusiasm to the Santa Clara stadium and to watch parties. “It means much more to us than just a game, because we are a small country, we went through hell, during the war, and now this just freedom for us to show love for the countries,” Tucakovic said. Santa Clara’s Euro Grill is planning a special menu for a watch party and offering courtesy drinks to fans. “I’m hoping that at least a small part of them will find their way to our restaurant, so we can share that joy,” Mirza Avviz with Euro Grill said. The owners proudly display mementos from the Sarajevo Olympics and their team’s colors, but they also say they will not be rooting against the U.S. team, from their second home. “People who live here are proud to be part of both countries. Of course, Bosnia Herzegovina that’s our Mother Land, but this is our country right now,” Avviz said. Some of the fans from both teams have been traveling across North America to see their teams play. And this is a home game for those fans from the Bay Area, so all of these watch parties should be held on July 1. The Golden Gate Restaurant Association also said they are helping to set up more watch parties for The American Outlaws and Mexico and Argentina fans.

headlines.title: Privacy group issues safety advisory over ID scanners at San Francisco gay bars
headlines.description: A national digital privacy group has issued a safety advisory ahead of Pride weekend, warning patrons about ID-scanning kiosks used at several popular nightclubs in San Francisco’s Castro neighborhood. The devices, manufactured by the Canadian company Patronscan, require door staff to scan a customer’s ID and take a digital photograph before allowing entry. The kiosks are currently in use at Badlands, Toad Hall, and The Mix. While advocates argue the technology poses a significant risk to the LGBTQIA+ community by unnecessarily tracking personal data, bar management insists the scanners are vital tools to keep patrons and staff safe following a violent attack last year. Fight for the Future, a digital rights advocacy group, issued the advisory specifically naming the three Castro bars and urging Pride weekend attendees to make informed choices about where they celebrate. “It’s really not a safe time to start making databases of queer people that could be accessed by the Trump administration, by a private company, by a bad actor,” said Evan Greer with Fight for the Future. Greer noted that similar privacy concerns surrounding Patronscan have been raised at other major venues, including Madison Square Garden in New York. Eric Norman, a frequent patron of The Mix, said his initial annoyance with the scanning process evolved into genuine concern as he learned more about the technology and its potential implications. “This is way more than just verifying my ID,” Norman said. “The more I learned, it went from me being annoyed to an actual concern and feeling like we shouldn’t have this technology in use.” Despite the pushback, the general manager of The Mix, identified only as Nick, vigorously defended the technology. He said the club installed the system after a customer followed an employee after their shift and brutally attacked them near the Castro Theatre last April. “It keeps my customers safe. We’ve had no bar fights, vandalism and theft is down,” the manager said. He added that the bar would drop the product immediately if it found out the company was sharing data with a third party. In a detailed statement and blog post, a Patronscan spokesperson pushed back against the privacy claims, stating their system does not use facial recognition or collect biometric data. Furthermore, the company stated that to comply with state law, it deletes all ID and photo data in California after 21 days. While the debate over the technology’s place in the Castro continues, patrons like Norman are calling for open dialogue with business owners and city government rather than organizing protests. “I would never encourage anyone to not go into a bar,” Norman said. “I would encourage people to politely articulate, ‘I have a problem with this software.'”

headlines.title: Chemical odor prompts shelter-in-place order in Walnut Creek
headlines.description: A chemical odor in Walnut Creek prompted a shelter-in-place order Friday, according to authorities. The Contra Costa County Fire Protection District said the incident was reported at 4:48 p.m. at 2020 Gill Port Lane in Walnut Creek. The incident was previously reported as a hazardous chemical spill, but fire officials later clarified that a chemical odor was what triggered the alert, which has since been lifted. Authorities said the odor was linked to a problem with a backyard pool at one of the homes.

headlines.title: Vandal shatters windows at Vallejo gay bar 2 nights in a row
headlines.description: Solano County’s only gay bar was vandalized on consecutive nights this week, causing thousands of dollars in damage in what the owners believe were targeted attacks ahead of regional Pride events. Surveillance cameras captured a masked suspect in a hooded sweatshirt using a large metal padlock to smash the front glass door and windows at Town House Cocktail Lounge shortly after the bar closed at 1 a.m. Thursday. Nobody was inside at the time, and the owners decided to leave the padlock in the window to avoid disturbing the scene. The destruction continued early Friday morning when someone — possibly the same person — shattered the side windows of a neighboring storefront rented out by the bar as a leather workshop, where Pride flags were draped inside. “We actually heard the guy last night,” said Jeff Linn, who co-owns the lounge with his partner, Andy Pembroke, and lives nearby. “I threw on my clothes as quickly as possible and ran downstairs and raced outside and screamed at him.” Vallejo police are investigating the back-to-back attacks. Vallejo Police Sgt. Rashad Hollis confirmed the investigation but said as of now, “there are no elements of a hate crime.” Linn said he feels safe in Vallejo and hopes the incident is just petty crime, but both owners suspect the business was targeted because it is a gay bar. “I hate to call it a hate crime, but it feels like it and it feels like there’s a lot of intent,” Linn said. Pembroke and Linn bought the bar in 2020 and opened it in 2021. Since then, the business has grown exponentially, drawing crowds for karaoke and drag nights and becoming a safe space for the local LGBTQIA+ community. “There are a lot of Pride events going on, so that could be their frame of mind,” Pembroke said. Despite the attacks, the owners are determined to keep their doors open. “I don’t wanna give into what they’re trying to accomplish,” Pembroke said. “They wanna smash our windows, all right, we’ll wake up, clean up the glass, move on.” Linn estimated the window repairs will cost about $4,500. A GoFundMe campaign established to help the business cover the costs had raised more than $2,200 as of Friday afternoon. The owners had planned to attend Pride events around the Bay Area this weekend, including San Francisco’s annual Pride parade, which draws tens of thousands of people to celebrate LGBTQIA+ life. “I don’t know if we will go now,” Linn said.
headlines.source: Jodi Hernandez and Bay City News | Griffin Jones
